message:
feat(tests): add DDL/DML identifier quoting integration tests (bd-2vvw)

Add 39 comprehensive integration tests for identifier quoting across
DDL and DML generation, covering:

DDL Tests (create.rs - 12 tests):
- SQL keyword table/column names (order, select, user, etc.)
- Embedded quotes in identifiers (doubled quote escaping)
- Unicode table/column names (CJK, emoji)
- Spaces in identifiers
- FK references to keyword tables
- Unique constraints with keyword columns
- Validation of 40+ SQL reserved words
- Edge cases (empty string, backslash, consecutive quotes)

DROP TABLE Tests (lib.rs - 7 tests):
- Added drop_table_sql() helper for testable SQL generation
- Keyword table names, embedded quotes, unicode, spaces
- SQL injection attempt neutralization

DML Tests (flush.rs - 20 tests):
- Added to_sql() methods to LinkTableOp and PendingOp
- INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with keyword table/column names
- Embedded quote escaping in all DML operations
- Composite primary key handling
- SQL injection neutralization tests

Closes bd-2vvw, completes EPIC bd-ymj3.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2q3","title":"Implement lazy loading for relationships","description":"## Description

Implement lazy loading semantics for relationships in Rust.

## Python Behavior

```python
class Team(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    heroes: List['Hero'] = Relationship(back_populates='team')

# Lazy loading - heroes loaded on first access
team = session.get(Team, 1)
print(team.heroes)  # SQL query executed HERE
```

## Rust Approach

Since Rust doesn't have implicit property access hooks, we use explicit loading:

### Option 1: LazyRelated<T> wrapper
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Team {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, lazy)]
    heroes: LazyRelated<Vec<Hero>>,
}

impl Team {
    async fn heroes(&self, cx: &Cx, session: &Session) -> Outcome<&[Hero], Error> {
        self.heroes.load(cx, session).await
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Session method
```rust
let heroes = session.load_related(cx, &team, Team::heroes).await?;
```

### Option 3: Auto-load configuration
```rust
#[sqlmodel(relationship, load = "auto")]
heroes: Related<Vec<Hero>>,
```

## Requirements

1. Track which relationships are loaded
2. Load on explicit request
3. Support batch loading to avoid N+1
4. Cache loaded relationships
5. Clear cache on refresh

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] LazyRelated<T> type implemented
- [ ] Load method fetches from database
- [ ] Loaded state tracked
- [ ] Works with Session context
- [ ] N+1 detection warns if many individual loads

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/lazy.rs)
- [ ] Test lazy relationship not loaded initially
- [ ] Test access triggers load
- [ ] Test loaded state persists
- [ ] Test lazy with session closed errors gracefully
- [ ] Test eager load override

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/lazy_loading.rs)
- [ ] Access hero.team → triggers SELECT on team
- [ ] Multiple access → single query
- [ ] Lazy load after detach → error
- [ ] Batch lazy loading (N+1 prevention)
- [ ] Lazy load with filters

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Lazy attribute access detected
- [ ] DEBUG: Lazy load query issued
- [ ] WARN: N+1 query pattern detected

 {"id":"bd-2sh","title":"Phase 10: Documentation and User Guides","description":"## Purpose
Create comprehensive documentation for the console integration, covering API reference, user guides, agent compatibility notes, and integration patterns.

## Background
Good documentation ensures:
- Users can quickly get started
- Advanced features are discoverable
- Agent authors understand compatibility
- Contributors understand the design

## Key Deliverables

### 1. API Documentation
- Rustdoc for all public types and traits
- Example code in doc comments
- Links between related items
- Feature flag documentation

### 2. Console User Guide
- Quick start (5-minute setup)
- Output mode configuration
- Theme customization
- Renderable catalog with examples

### 3. Agent Compatibility Guide
- How agent detection works
- Environment variables reference
- Stream separation semantics
- Writing agent-compatible code

### 4. Integration Patterns
- Attaching console to sessions
- Global vs explicit console
- Custom renderable creation
- Performance considerations

### 5. README Updates
- Update main sqlmodel README
- Add console feature section
- Include screenshots of output
- Link to detailed docs

## Documentation Locations
- crates/sqlmodel-console/README.md - crate overview
- docs/console/ - detailed guides
- Rustdoc in source files - API reference
- examples/ - runnable examples with comments

## Writing Standards
- Concise, action-oriented
- Code examples for everything
- Both rich and plain output shown
- Agent considerations always noted

## Dependencies
- All console features complete
- Visual examples finalized
- API stable

## Verification
- cargo doc builds without warnings
- All public items documented
- Examples in docs compile
- User guide tested by newcomer","acceptance_criteria":"API documentation covers all public types and functions
Console User Guide explains all features with examples
Agent Compatibility Guide documents agent detection
Main README updated with console feature documentation
All doc examples compile and run correctly
Documentation includes troubleshooting section","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:16:36.000228819Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T07:03:40.680681009Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T07:03:40.680552800Z","close_reason":"Phase 10 complete: User Guide, Agent Compatibility Guide, README section all implemented","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2sh","depends_on_id":"bd-18z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2sh","depends_on_id":"bd-eqb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":16,"issue_id":"bd-2sh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All public items have rustdoc with examples
- [ ] Console User Guide covers setup to advanced usage
- [ ] Agent Compatibility Guide explains stream separation
- [ ] README updated with console feature section
- [ ] Screenshots of rich output included in docs
- [ ] cargo doc builds without warnings
- [ ] All doc examples compile","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:37:38Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2ux","title":"Implement Session.refresh()","description":"## Description

Immediately reload object from database.

## Python Behavior

```python
# Reload all attributes
session.refresh(user)

# Reload specific attributes
session.refresh(user, ['name', 'email'])

# With options (eager load relationships)
session.refresh(user, with_for_update=True)
```

Unlike expire(), refresh() immediately queries the DB.

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub async fn refresh<M: Model>(
        &mut self,
        cx: &Cx,
        model: &mut M,
        attributes: Option<&[&str]>,
    ) -> Outcome<(), Error> {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        
        // Query database
        let fresh: M = select!(M)
            .filter(M::primary_key().eq(pk))
            .one(cx, &self.conn)
            .await?;
        
        // Update model
        match attributes {
            Some(attrs) => model.copy_attributes_from(&fresh, attrs),
            None => *model = fresh,
        }
        
        // Update identity map
        self.identity_map.update(model);
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Immediately queries database
- [ ] Updates model with fresh values
- [ ] Can refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] Updates identity map
- [ ] Errors if object not in DB (was deleted)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/refresh.rs)
- [ ] Test refresh reloads from DB
- [ ] Test refresh with attribute subset
- [ ] Test refresh clears pending changes
- [ ] Test refresh updates identity map
- [ ] Test refresh with relationships

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_refresh.rs)
- [ ] Modify externally → refresh → sees new values
- [ ] refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] refresh with eager-loaded relationships
- [ ] refresh after expire
- [ ] refresh detached object fails

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Refresh query issued
- [ ] TRACE: Fields refreshed

-{"id":"bd-2vvw","title":"Add DDL identifier quoting integration tests","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:11.186352588Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:19.499580712Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2vvw","depends_on_id":"bd-14xl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:12.477828372Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2vvw","depends_on_id":"bd-3sx5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:13.135349826Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2vvw","depends_on_id":"bd-ymj3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:11.192985756Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":60,"issue_id":"bd-2vvw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## What

Add integration tests that verify SQL identifier quoting works end-to-end for pathological table/column names.

## Where

New test module in crates/sqlmodel-schema/src/create.rs or a dedicated test file.

## Test Cases

1. Table name with embedded double-quote: `user"table` → `"user""table"`
2. Table name that is a SQL keyword: `select` → `"select"`
3. Column name with spaces: `first name` → `"first name"`
4. Empty table name → error (not empty quotes)
5. Table name with null byte → error or stripped
6. Unicode table name: `用户` → `"用户"`
7. DROP TABLE with quoted name: verify the SQL is syntactically correct
8. CREATE TABLE with quoted column names
9. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with quoted identifiers
10. MySQL backtick quoting equivalents

## Acceptance Criteria

- All 10+ test cases pass
- Tests run in cargo test --all
- No new dependencies added
","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:11Z"},{"id":63,"issue_id":"bd-2vvw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## What

Write comprehensive unit tests and integration tests verifying SQL identifier quoting works correctly across all crates — the shared utility, the schema DDL, the session flush DML, and the migration runner.

## Where

Files:
- crates/sqlmodel-core/src/types.rs or identifiers.rs — unit tests for quote_ident()
- crates/sqlmodel-schema/src/create.rs — integration tests for DDL generation
- crates/sqlmodel-schema/src/lib.rs — integration tests for drop_table()
- crates/sqlmodel-session/src/flush.rs — integration tests for DML generation
- crates/sqlmodel-schema/src/introspect.rs — verify sanitize_identifier works

## Unit Tests for quote_ident() Utility

### ANSI Double-Quote Style
1. `test_quote_ident_simple` — `quote_ident("users")` → `"\"users\""`
2. `test_quote_ident_empty` — `quote_ident("")` → `"\"\""` (valid SQL, means empty-string identifier)
3. `test_quote_ident_embedded_double_quote` — `quote_ident("user\"name")` → `"\"user\"\"name\""`
4. `test_quote_ident_multiple_quotes` — `quote_ident("a\"b\"c")` → `"\"a\"\"b\"\"c\""`
5. `test_quote_ident_sql_keyword` — `quote_ident("select")` → `"\"select\""` (safe from keyword collision)
6. `test_quote_ident_spaces` — `quote_ident("first name")` → `"\"first name\""`
7. `test_quote_ident_unicode` — `quote_ident("用户")` → `"\"用户\""`
8. `test_quote_ident_semicolon` — `quote_ident("a;b")` → `"\"a;b\""` (semicolons inside quotes are safe)
9. `test_quote_ident_newline` — `quote_ident("a\nb")` → `"\"a\nb\""` (newlines inside quotes are safe)
10. `test_quote_ident_null_byte` — `quote_ident("a\0b")` → should either strip the null or error (discuss)
11. `test_quote_ident_backslash` — `quote_ident("a\\b")` → `"\"a\\b\""` (backslash is literal in ANSI SQL)
12. `test_quote_ident_only_quotes` — `quote_ident("\"\"")` → `"\"\"\"\"\"\"\""`

### MySQL Backtick Style
13. `test_quote_ident_mysql_simple` — `quote_ident_mysql("users")` → `` "`users`" ``
14. `test_quote_ident_mysql_embedded_backtick` — `quote_ident_mysql("user`name")` → `` "`user``name`" ``
15. `test_quote_ident_mysql_keyword` — `quote_ident_mysql("select")` → `` "`select`" ``

### sanitize_identifier Tests
16. `test_sanitize_simple` — `sanitize_identifier("users")` → `"users"`
17. `test_sanitize_strips_quotes` — `sanitize_identifier("user\"name")` → `"username"`
18. `test_sanitize_strips_semicolons` — `sanitize_identifier("a;b")` → `"ab"`
19. `test_sanitize_preserves_underscore` — `sanitize_identifier("user_name")` → `"user_name"`
20. `test_sanitize_strips_spaces` — `sanitize_identifier("user name")` → `"username"`
21. `test_sanitize_empty_input` — `sanitize_identifier("")` → `""` (caller should handle)

## Integration Tests for DDL Generation

22. `test_create_table_with_quoted_name` — CREATE TABLE with keyword name generates valid SQL
23. `test_create_table_with_quoted_columns` — columns with spaces/keywords are properly quoted
24. `test_drop_table_with_quoted_name` — DROP TABLE properly quotes the identifier
25. `test_drop_table_with_embedded_quote` — table name containing `"` is properly escaped
26. `test_migration_table_name_sanitized` — migration runner sanitizes custom table names

## Integration Tests for DML Generation (Flush)

27. `test_flush_insert_quotes_table_and_columns` — generated INSERT SQL has properly quoted identifiers
28. `test_flush_update_quotes_table_and_columns` — generated UPDATE SQL has properly quoted identifiers
29. `test_flush_delete_quotes_table` — generated DELETE SQL has properly quoted table name
30. `test_flush_link_insert_quotes_identifiers` — link table INSERT quotes all identifiers
31. `test_flush_link_delete_quotes_identifiers` — link table DELETE quotes all identifiers

## End-to-End Scenario Tests

32. `test_roundtrip_with_keyword_table_name` — Create a model with table name "order", create table, insert, select, drop — all SQL is valid
33. `test_roundtrip_with_quoted_column` — Column named "group" works end-to-end
34. `test_sql_injection_attempt_neutralized` — Table name `"users\"; DROP TABLE secrets; --"` is safely quoted and doesn't cause injection

## Acceptance Criteria

- At least 34 tests across utility, DDL, DML, and E2E levels
- All identifier quoting edge cases (quotes, keywords, unicode, null bytes) covered
- Both ANSI and MySQL quoting styles tested
- SQL injection attempts demonstrated to be neutralized
- All tests pass with `cargo test --all`
- Tests have clear comments explaining what each edge case tests and why
","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:19Z"}]}

## Test Cases

1. Table name with embedded double-quote: `user"table` → `"user""table"`
2. Table name that is a SQL keyword: `select` → `"select"`
3. Column name with spaces: `first name` → `"first name"`
4. Empty table name → error (not empty quotes)
5. Table name with null byte → error or stripped
6. Unicode table name: `用户` → `"用户"`
7. DROP TABLE with quoted name: verify the SQL is syntactically correct
8. CREATE TABLE with quoted column names
9. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with quoted identifiers
10. MySQL backtick quoting equivalents

## Acceptance Criteria

- All 10+ test cases pass
- Tests run in cargo test --all
- No new dependencies added

## Integration Tests for DDL Generation

## Integration Tests for DML Generation (Flush)

27. `test_flush_insert_quotes_table_and_columns` — generated INSERT SQL has properly quoted identifiers

 {"id":"bd-2x6","title":"Implement passive_deletes for relationships","description":"## Description

Support passive_deletes to let database handle cascade deletes.

## Python Behavior

```python
class Parent(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    children: List['Child'] = Relationship(
        back_populates='parent',
        passive_deletes=True  # Let DB handle deletes via FK ON DELETE
    )
    
    # With 'all', don't even warn about orphans
    children2: List['Child'] = Relationship(passive_deletes='all')
```

When passive_deletes=True:
- SQLAlchemy doesn't emit DELETE for children
- Relies on DB's ON DELETE CASCADE
- More efficient for large collections

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Parent {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes)]
    children: Related<Vec<Child>>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes = "all")]
    children2: Related<Vec<Child>>,
}
```

## Behavior

- passive_deletes=false (default): ORM deletes related objects
- passive_deletes=true: ORM relies on DB cascade
- passive_deletes='all': Don't even track orphans

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] passive_deletes attribute parsed
- [ ] Session.delete() respects passive_deletes
- [ ] Works with ON DELETE CASCADE FK constraint
- [ ] 'all' variant prevents orphan tracking

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/relationship.rs)
- [ ] Test passive_deletes=True parsed
- [ ] Test passive_deletes="all" parsed
- [ ] Test affects ON DELETE behavior
- [ ] Test with different FK constraints

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/passive_deletes.rs)
- [ ] Delete parent → DB cascades children (passive)
- [ ] passive_deletes="all" for all operations
- [ ] passive_deletes + ON DELETE CASCADE
- [ ] No orphan cleanup with passive
- [ ] Compare active vs passive delete behavior

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: passive_deletes configuration
- [ ] TRACE: Delete cascade behavior
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:04:24.009285380Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:44:20.215593056Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T22:44:20.215452194Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2x6","depends_on_id":"bd-1u7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:56:56.295999170Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2ybr","title":"Implement built-in validators (uuid, ipv4, ipv6, mac_address)","description":"## Description

Add common validation patterns as built-in validators.

## Python Validators

```python
from pydantic import Field

class Network(SQLModel):
    device_id: str = Field(pattern=r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-...$')  # UUID pattern
    ipv4: str  # IPv4Address type does validation
    ipv6: str  # IPv6Address type does validation  
    mac: str = Field(pattern=r'^([0-9A-Fa-f]{2}:){5}[0-9A-Fa-f]{2}$')
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
struct Network {
    #[validate(uuid)]
    device_id: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv4)]
    ipv4: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv6)]
    ipv6: String,
    
    #[validate(mac_address)]
    mac: String,
    
    #[validate(slug)]
    url_slug: String,
    
    #[validate(credit_card)]
    card_number: String,
}
```

## Built-in Patterns

- uuid: UUID format
- ipv4: IPv4 address
- ipv6: IPv6 address
- mac_address: MAC address
- slug: URL-safe slug
- credit_card: Credit card number (Luhn check)
- phone: Phone number (E.164)
- hex_color: Hex color code

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] uuid validator
- [ ] ipv4 validator
- [ ] ipv6 validator
- [ ] mac_address validator
- [ ] slug validator
- [ ] credit_card validator (Luhn)
- [ ] Clear error messages for each

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/validate.rs)
- [ ] Test uuid validator accepts valid UUID
- [ ] Test uuid validator rejects invalid
- [ ] Test ipv4 validator
- [ ] Test ipv6 validator
- [ ] Test mac_address validator
- [ ] Test custom format validators

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/builtin_validators.rs)
- [ ] Field with uuid validation
- [ ] Field with ipv4 validation
- [ ] Field with ipv6 validation
- [ ] Field with mac_address validation
- [ ] Combined validators on one field
- [ ] Custom format registration

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Validator pattern matching
- [ ] TRACE: Validation failure details

 {"id":"bd-x1x","title":"Integrate prepared statement protocol into MySqlAsyncConnection","description":"The prepared statement binary protocol is implemented in protocol/prepared.rs (bd-1a4 complete), but the MySqlAsyncConnection methods (prepare, query_prepared, execute_prepared) are currently stubs.

## Implementation Needed

1. **prepare()**: Send COM_STMT_PREPARE, parse response, return PreparedStatement
2. **query_prepared()**: Send COM_STMT_EXECUTE, parse binary result set
3. **execute_prepared()**: Same as query_prepared but return affected rows
4. **Close statements**: Track open statement IDs, send COM_STMT_CLOSE on connection close

 {"id":"bd-y77","title":"Convert sqlmodel-mysql to async Connection trait","description":"The MySQL driver has complete wire protocol implementation but is synchronous only. Migration needed:
1. Replace std::net::TcpStream with asupersync::net::TcpStream
2. Convert read_packet/write_packet to async with .await
3. Add &Cx context parameter to all async methods
4. Return Outcome<T, E> instead of Result<T, E>
5. Add cancellation checks via cx.checkpoint()

This enables MySQL query execution with the rest of the stack (Session, Pool, etc).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-28T02:24:18.036923422Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:25:13.263648906Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:25:13.263586199Z","close_reason":"Already implemented - MySqlAsyncConnection and SharedMySqlConnection both implement Connection trait","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-ymj3","title":"EPIC: DDL Identifier Quoting Safety — consistent SQL identifier escaping across all crates","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:04.823673709Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:05.473485292Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":56,"issue_id":"bd-ymj3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Background

Multiple locations across the schema and introspection crates construct SQL DDL statements by interpolating identifier names (table names, column names) directly into SQL via format!(). While some locations use sanitization (sanitize_table_name, sanitize_identifier) or quoting (quote_ident), the approach is inconsistent:

1. **sqlmodel-schema/src/lib.rs drop_table()**: Recently fixed to use quote-escaping, but the fix is ad-hoc (manual replace)
2. **sqlmodel-schema/src/migrate.rs**: Uses sanitize_table_name() (strips to alphanumeric+underscore) — effective but lossy
3. **sqlmodel-schema/src/introspect.rs**: Uses sanitize_identifier() for PRAGMA/SHOW commands — necessary since these don't support parameterization
4. **sqlmodel-schema/src/create.rs**: Uses quote_ident() — the gold standard approach
5. **sqlmodel-session/src/flush.rs**: Uses double-quoting for table/column names in DML

## The Problem

There is no single, consistent identifier-quoting utility used across all crates. The create.rs has `quote_ident()` but other files roll their own ad-hoc escaping. This is a defense-in-depth issue:
- Table/column names come from Model trait impls (compile-time static strings), so SQL injection risk is low
- BUT if dynamic models or user-provided table names are used, the inconsistent quoting becomes a real vulnerability
- Code duplication means bugs in quoting logic get fixed in one place but not others

## Strategy

1. Create a shared `quote_ident()` utility in sqlmodel-core (or sqlmodel-query)
2. Audit and replace all ad-hoc quoting/sanitization with the shared utility
3. Add a `sanitize_identifier()` for contexts where quoting isn't possible (PRAGMA, SHOW)
4. Add tests covering edge cases: embedded quotes, unicode, empty strings, reserved words

## Why P2

The current code is safe for the common case (Model-derived static strings). The risk exists only for dynamic/user-provided identifiers. Important to fix but not urgent.
","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:05Z"}]}

Multiple locations across the schema and introspection crates construct SQL DDL statements by interpolating identifier names (table names, column names) directly into SQL via format!(). While some locations use sanitization (sanitize_table_name, sanitize_identifier) or quoting (quote_ident), the approach is inconsistent:

1. **sqlmodel-schema/src/lib.rs drop_table()**: Recently fixed to use quote-escaping, but the fix is ad-hoc (manual replace)
2. **sqlmodel-schema/src/migrate.rs**: Uses sanitize_table_name() (strips to alphanumeric+underscore) — effective but lossy
3. **sqlmodel-schema/src/introspect.rs**: Uses sanitize_identifier() for PRAGMA/SHOW commands — necessary since these don't support parameterization
4. **sqlmodel-schema/src/create.rs**: Uses quote_ident() — the gold standard approach
5. **sqlmodel-session/src/flush.rs**: Uses double-quoting for table/column names in DML

## The Problem

There is no single, consistent identifier-quoting utility used across all crates. The create.rs has `quote_ident()` but other files roll their own ad-hoc escaping. This is a defense-in-depth issue:
- Table/column names come from Model trait impls (compile-time static strings), so SQL injection risk is low
- BUT if dynamic models or user-provided table names are used, the inconsistent quoting becomes a real vulnerability
- Code duplication means bugs in quoting logic get fixed in one place but not others

## Strategy

1. Create a shared `quote_ident()` utility in sqlmodel-core (or sqlmodel-query)
2. Audit and replace all ad-hoc quoting/sanitization with the shared utility
3. Add a `sanitize_identifier()` for contexts where quoting isn't possible (PRAGMA, SHOW)
4. Add tests covering edge cases: embedded quotes, unicode, empty strings, reserved words

## Why P2

The current code is safe for the common case (Model-derived static strings). The risk exists only for dynamic/user-provided identifiers. Important to fix but not urgent.

 {"id":"bd-zhp","title":"Implement Theme struct with SQLModel color palette","description":"# Implement Theme Struct with SQLModel Color Palette

## Task Description

Create a Theme struct that defines the color palette used throughout sqlmodel_rust
console output. This ensures visual consistency across all renderables.

## File: src/theme.rs

```rust
//! Theme definitions for SQLModel console output.
//!
//! Provides a consistent color palette for all styled output.

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::style::{Color, Style};

/// SQLModel console theme with semantic colors.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Theme {
    // === Status Colors ===
    /// Success messages, completion indicators
    pub success: ThemeColor,
    /// Error messages, failure indicators  
    pub error: ThemeColor,
    /// Warning messages, deprecation notices
    pub warning: ThemeColor,
    /// Informational messages, hints
    pub info: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Value Type Colors ===
    /// NULL values (typically dim/italic)
    pub null_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Boolean values (true/false)
    pub bool_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Numeric values (integers, floats)
    pub number_value: ThemeColor,
    /// String/text values
    pub string_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Date/time/timestamp values
    pub date_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Binary/blob values
    pub binary_value: ThemeColor,
    /// JSON values
    pub json_value: ThemeColor,
    /// UUID values
    pub uuid_value: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Syntax Colors ===
    /// SQL keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE)
    pub sql_keyword: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL strings ('value')
    pub sql_string: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL numbers (42, 3.14)
    pub sql_number: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL comments (-- comment)
    pub sql_comment: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL operators (=, >, AND)
    pub sql_operator: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL identifiers (table names, column names)
    pub sql_identifier: ThemeColor,
    
    // === UI Element Colors ===
    /// Table/panel borders
    pub border: ThemeColor,
    /// Headers and titles
    pub header: ThemeColor,
    /// Dimmed/secondary text
    pub dim: ThemeColor,
    /// Highlighted/emphasized text
    pub highlight: ThemeColor,
}

/// A color that can be rendered differently based on mode.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct ThemeColor {
    /// RGB color for rich mode (r, g, b)
    pub rgb: (u8, u8, u8),
    /// ANSI 256-color fallback
    pub ansi256: u8,
    /// Plain text marker (for plain mode output)
    pub plain_marker: Option<&'static str>,
}

impl ThemeColor {
    /// Create a theme color with RGB and ANSI256 fallback.
    pub const fn new(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: None }
    }
    
    /// Create with a plain text marker for non-color output.
    pub const fn with_marker(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8, marker: &'static str) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: Some(marker) }
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Color (truecolor).
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_color(&self) -> Color {
        Color::from_rgb(self.rgb.0, self.rgb.1, self.rgb.2)
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Style with this as foreground.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_style(&self) -> Style {
        Style::new().color(self.to_color())
    }
}

impl Theme {
    /// Create the default dark theme (Dracula-inspired).
    pub fn dark() -> Self {
        Self {
            // Status colors (Dracula palette)
            success: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),      // Green
            error: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),        // Red
            warning: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),    // Yellow
            info: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117),       // Cyan
            
            // Value type colors
            null_value: ThemeColor::with_marker((98, 114, 164), 60, "NULL"),
            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),  // Yellow
            number_value: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117), // Cyan
            string_value: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),   // Green
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),   // Magenta
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215), // Orange
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215),   // Orange
            
            // SQL syntax colors
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),  // Magenta
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),     // Green
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),    // Gray
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),   // Red
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255), // White
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),        // Gray
            header: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255),      // White
            dim: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),           // Gray
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((255, 255, 255), 231),   // Bright white
        }
    }
    
    /// Create a light theme variant.
    pub fn light() -> Self {
        Self {
            // Status colors (adjusted for light background)
            success: ThemeColor::new((40, 167, 69), 34),
            error: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            warning: ThemeColor::new((255, 193, 7), 220),
            info: ThemeColor::new((23, 162, 184), 37),
            
            // Value colors (darker for visibility on light bg)
            null_value: ThemeColor::with_marker((108, 117, 125), 244, "NULL"),
            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            number_value: ThemeColor::new((0, 150, 136), 30),
            string_value: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            
            // SQL syntax (darker)
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            header: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            dim: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((0, 0, 0), 16),
        }
    }
}

impl Default for Theme {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::dark()
    }
}
```

## Color Philosophy

### Dracula Palette Base
The dark theme uses colors from the Dracula palette because:
1. It's widely recognized and loved by developers
2. High contrast ratios for accessibility
3. Distinct colors for each semantic meaning
4. Looks professional in terminal screenshots

### Semantic Consistency
- **Green** = Success, strings (positive/data)
- **Red** = Errors, operators (danger/action)
- **Yellow** = Warnings, booleans (caution/special)
- **Cyan** = Info, numbers (neutral data)
- **Magenta** = Dates, SQL keywords (special syntax)
- **Purple** = JSON, SQL numbers (structured data)
- **Gray** = Dim text, comments, borders (secondary)

### Accessibility
- All colors chosen to meet WCAG contrast guidelines
- ANSI256 fallbacks for terminals without truecolor
- Plain markers for complete accessibility

## Verification

```bash
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features rich
cargo test -p sqlmodel-console theme::tests
```","acceptance_criteria":"Theme struct has dark and light variants
ThemeColor enum supports RGB, ANSI256, and basic colors
Theme provides semantic colors for all SQL elements
Theme::from_env() detects user preference
All unit tests pass for theme functionality
Theme colors render correctly in both Rich and Plain modes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:05:02.295059680Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T09:12:31.070100671Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T09:12:31.069985544Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["phase-2","rich-rust","theme"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-zhp","depends_on_id":"bd-1ob","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-zhp","depends_on_id":"bd-25i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":34,"issue_id":"bd-zhp","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Required Unit Tests

1. test_dark_theme_creation - verify Theme::dark() returns valid theme
2. test_light_theme_creation - verify Theme::light() returns valid theme
3. test_default_theme_is_dark - verify Default trait uses dark theme
4. test_theme_color_rgb_values - verify RGB values are valid (0-255)
5. test_theme_color_ansi256_values - verify ANSI256 values are valid (0-255)
6. test_theme_color_to_style - verify ThemeColor::to_style() creates valid Style
7. test_status_colors_distinct - verify success/error/warning/info are distinguishable
8. test_sql_value_colors_complete - verify all SQL types have colors

10. test_theme_color_with_marker - verify plain_marker field works","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:35:39Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-zy3","title":"Implement Session.is_modified() for dirty checking","description":"## Description

Check if an object has pending changes.

## Python Behavior

```python
user.name = 'New Name'
session.is_modified(user)  # True

session.is_modified(user, include_collections=False)  # Check attrs only
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub fn is_modified<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> bool {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        if let Some(state) = self.get_state::<M>(&pk) {
            return state.is_dirty();
        }
        false
    }
    
    pub fn modified_attributes<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> Vec<&str> {
        // Return list of changed attribute names
    }
}
```

## Dirty Tracking

Need to track original values:
```rust
struct ObjectState {
    original_values: HashMap<String, Value>,
    pending: ObjectPendingState,
}

enum ObjectPendingState {
    New,
    Clean,
    Dirty { changed: HashSet<String> },
    Deleted,
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Returns true if object has unsaved changes
- [ ] Returns false for clean objects
- [ ] Works with new (pending) objects
- [ ] Can check collections separately
- [ ] modified_attributes() returns changed field names

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/dirty.rs)
- [ ] Test is_modified for unchanged object
- [ ] Test is_modified for changed field
- [ ] Test is_modified for relationship change
- [ ] Test is_modified for new object
- [ ] Test is_modified for deleted object

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_dirty.rs)
- [ ] Load object → is_modified=false
- [ ] Change field → is_modified=true
- [ ] Flush → is_modified=false again
- [ ] is_modified with nested objects
- [ ] Specific field dirty check

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Dirty field detection
- [ ] DEBUG: Dirty state changes

 {"id":"sqlmodel_rust-0gv","title":"SQLModel Rust: MySQL Protocol Implementation","description":"# Epic: MySQL Protocol Implementation (sqlmodel-mysql)

## Overview
This epic implements the MySQL wire protocol from scratch. MySQL uses a different protocol than PostgreSQL but follows similar principles: TCP connection, length-prefixed packets, and request-response patterns.

## Rationale
Same reasoning as PostgreSQL: full control, native async, cancellation support, and first-principles understanding. MySQL is important for:
- Huge existing deployments
- AWS Aurora MySQL
- MariaDB compatibility
- Different feature set than PostgreSQL

## Key Components

### 1. Packet Protocol (protocol.rs)
MySQL uses 4-byte header packets:
- 3 bytes: payload length
- 1 byte: sequence number
- Payload

Packets over 16MB are split. Sequence numbers track request/response pairing.

### 2. Connection Handshake (handshake.rs)
Initial handshake:
1. Server sends HandshakeV10:
   - Protocol version
   - Server version string
   - Connection ID
   - Auth plugin data (salt)
   - Capability flags
   - Character set
   - Status flags
2. Client sends HandshakeResponse:
   - Capability flags
   - Max packet size
   - Character set
   - Username, auth response
   - Database name
   - Auth plugin name

### 3. Authentication (auth.rs)
MySQL auth plugins:
- mysql_native_password: SHA1-based (legacy)
- caching_sha2_password: SHA256-based (default MySQL 8.0+)
- sha256_password: RSA-based
- auth_gssapi_client: Kerberos

caching_sha2_password flow:
1. Client sends scrambled password
2. Server may request full auth (public key exchange)
3. Client encrypts password with server's public key
4. Server verifies

### 4. Command Protocol (commands.rs)
MySQL commands (COM_xxx):
- COM_QUERY: Text protocol query
- COM_STMT_PREPARE: Prepare statement
- COM_STMT_EXECUTE: Execute prepared
- COM_STMT_CLOSE: Close prepared
- COM_STMT_RESET: Reset statement
- COM_PING: Keep-alive
- COM_QUIT: Close connection
- COM_INIT_DB: Change database
- COM_FIELD_LIST: Get columns (deprecated)

### 5. Result Sets (results.rs)
Text protocol results:
1. Column count packet
2. Column definition packets (per column)
3. EOF packet (or OK if no results)
4. Row packets (length-encoded strings)
5. EOF packet (end of rows)

Binary protocol results (prepared statements):
- Similar structure but binary encoding
- NULL bitmap for nullable columns
- Type-specific binary encoding

### 6. Type System (types.rs)
MySQL types to Rust:
- MYSQL_TYPE_TINY -> i8
- MYSQL_TYPE_SHORT -> i16
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONG -> i32
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONGLONG -> i64
- MYSQL_TYPE_FLOAT -> f32
- MYSQL_TYPE_DOUBLE -> f64
- MYSQL_TYPE_STRING, VARCHAR -> String
- MYSQL_TYPE_BLOB -> Vec<u8>
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATE -> date
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATETIME -> datetime
- MYSQL_TYPE_JSON -> serde_json::Value

Length-encoded integers:
- < 251: 1 byte
- < 2^16: 0xFC + 2 bytes
- < 2^24: 0xFD + 3 bytes
- < 2^64: 0xFE + 8 bytes

### 7. Prepared Statements (prepared.rs)
Binary protocol advantages:
- No SQL injection (parameters separate)
- Efficient binary encoding
- Server-side caching
- Correct type handling

Flow:
1. COM_STMT_PREPARE -> statement ID, param count, column count
2. COM_STMT_EXECUTE with binary parameters
3. Binary result set

### 8. Error Handling (error.rs)
ERR packet format:
- 0xFF marker
- Error code (2 bytes)
- SQL state marker '#'
- SQL state (5 bytes)
- Error message

Map MySQL error codes to meaningful errors.

### 9. Transactions
MySQL transaction commands:
- START TRANSACTION
- COMMIT, ROLLBACK
- SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT
- SET autocommit = 0/1

### 10. Character Sets
MySQL character set handling:
- Connection charset
- Result charset
- Collation

Default to utf8mb4 for full Unicode.

## Key Design Decisions
1. **Async native**: Use asupersync TCP throughout
2. **Capability negotiation**: Support both old and new features
3. **Auth plugin system**: Extensible auth mechanism
4. **Prepared statement cache**: Reuse prepared statements
5. **Connection reset**: Use COM_RESET_CONNECTION vs reconnect

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Connect to MySQL 5.7 and 8.0+
- [ ] All auth methods work
- [ ] Text protocol queries
- [ ] Binary protocol prepared statements
- [ ] All common types map correctly
- [ ] Transactions work correctly
- [ ] Error codes mapped to Rust errors
- [ ] SSL/TLS connections
- [ ] Connection pooling integration
- [ ] MariaDB compatibility
- [ ] Correct character set handling

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-core (Connection trait, Value, Row, Error)
- asupersync (TCP, cancellation, Budget)

## Estimated Scope
~2000 lines of protocol implementation

## References
- MySQL Protocol: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/dev/mysql-server/latest/PAGE_PROTOCOL.html
